lines.greek: ἔρχεο πευσόμενος πατρὸς δὴν οἰχομένοιο,
ἤν τίς τοι εἴπῃσι βροτῶν, ἢ ὄσσαν ἀκούσῃς
ἐκ Διός, ἥ τε μάλιστα φέρει κλέος ἀνθρώποισι.
πρῶτα μὲν ἐς Πύλον ἐλθὲ καὶ εἴρεο Νέστορα δῖον,
κεῖθεν δὲ Σπάρτηνδε παρὰ ξανθὸν Μενέλαον·
ὃς γὰρ δεύτατος ἦλθεν Ἀχαιῶν χαλκοχιτώνων.
εἰ μέν κεν πατρὸς βίοτον καὶ νόστον ἀκούσῃς,
ἦ τʼ ἂν τρυχόμενός περ ἔτι τλαίης ἐνιαυτόν·
εἰ δέ κε τεθνηῶτος ἀκούσῃς μηδʼ ἔτʼ ἐόντος,
νοστήσας δὴ ἔπειτα φίλην ἐς πατρίδα γαῖαν
σῆμά τέ οἱ χεῦαι καὶ ἐπὶ κτέρεα κτερεΐξαι
πολλὰ μάλʼ, ὅσσα ἔοικε, καὶ ἀνέρι μητέρα δοῦναι.
αὐτὰρ ἐπὴν δὴ ταῦτα τελευτήσῃς τε καὶ ἔρξῃς,
φράζεσθαι δὴ ἔπειτα κατὰ φρένα καὶ κατὰ θυμὸν
ὅππως κε μνηστῆρας ἐνὶ μεγάροισι τεοῖσι
κτείνῃς ἠὲ δόλῳ ἢ ἀμφαδόν· οὐδέ τί σε χρὴ
νηπιάας ὀχέειν, ἐπεὶ οὐκέτι τηλίκος ἐσσι.
ἢ οὐκ ἀίεις οἷον κλέος ἔλλαβε δῖος Ὀρέστης
πάντας ἐπʼ ἀνθρώπους, ἐπεὶ ἔκτανε πατροφονῆα,
Αἴγισθον δολόμητιν, ὅ οἱ πατέρα κλυτὸν ἔκτα;

interlinear: go, to-inquire of-your-father long departed,
if any of-mortals should-tell-you, or a-rumour you-should-hear
from Zeus, which especially brings report to-men.
first indeed to Pylos go, and ask goodly Nestor,
and-from-there to-Sparta, to fair-haired Menelaus;
for-he last came of-the-Achaeans, the-bronze-clad.
if indeed of-your-father's life and homecoming you-should-hear,
truly then, though-worn-down, still you-might-endure a-year;
but-if of-him-dead you-should-hear, and no-longer being,
returning then thereafter to-your-dear native land,
a-mound for-him heap-up, and funeral-rites perform,
very many, as-many-as befit, and to-a-husband your-mother give.
but when indeed these-things you-have-finished and done,
consider then thereafter in your-mind and in your-heart
how the-wooers in halls your-own
you-may-slay, either by-guile or openly; nor at-all must-you
childish-ways carry-on, since no-longer of-that-age are-you.
or have-you-not heard what renown won goodly Orestes
among all men, when he-slew his-father's-slayer,
Aegisthus the-guileful, who his father famous had-slain?
literal: and go to seek news of your father, long departed,
in case some mortal should tell you, or you should hear a rumour
from Zeus, which above all brings report to men.
First go to Pylos and ask goodly Nestor,
and from there to Sparta, to fair-haired Menelaus;
for he came home last of the bronze-clad Achaeans.
If you should hear that your father lives and is coming home,
then, worn down though you are, you might endure another year;
but if you should hear that he is dead and no longer alive,
then return to your own dear native land,
and heap up a mound for him, and perform funeral rites,
very many, as is fitting, and give your mother to a husband.
But when you have finished and done these things,
then consider in your mind and in your heart
how you may slay the wooers in your own halls,
whether by guile or openly; nor must you
carry on in childish ways, since you are no longer of that age.
Or have you not heard what renown goodly Orestes won
among all men, when he slew his father's slayer,
guileful Aegisthus, who had slain his famous father?
translations.elizabethan:
Then go and seek some news of thy lost father,
if any mortal tell thee, or if thou
shouldst catch a rumour out of Zeus, which most
of all brings tidings among men. First go
to Pylos, and ask goodly Nestor there;
from thence to Sparta, to the fair-haired lord
Menelaus, who came latest home of all
the bronze-clad Achaeans. If thou hear
thy father lives and is upon his way,
then, worn as thou art, endure another year.
But if thou hear that he is dead and gone,
return thee to thy own dear fatherland,
and heap a mound for him, and pay the rites,
full many, as is fitting, and bestow
thy mother on a husband. When all this
is finished and performed, then take thou thought
within thy mind and heart, by what device,
by guile or in the open, thou shalt kill
the suitors in thy halls. It is not fit
that thou go on in childish ways; thy years
are past that now. Hast thou not heard what fame
goodly Orestes won among all men,
when he cut down his father's murderer,
guileful Aegisthus, who had slain his sire?
translations.modernist:
Then go and look for news of your father. Somebody may tell you. Or you may catch a rumour out of Zeus, which is what carries word furthest among men.

Go to Pylos first and ask goodly Nestor. Then go on to Sparta, to fair-haired Menelaus. He came home last of all the bronze-clad Achaeans.

If you hear your father is alive and on his way, then hold out one more year, worn down as you are. If you hear he is dead and gone, come home to your own country. Heap up a mound for him. Do the rites, all of them, the way it should be done. Then give your mother to a husband.

When that is finished, think hard. Work out how you are going to kill the suitors in your own hall. By a trick or in the open. It does not matter which. You are too old to go on behaving like a child.

Have you not heard what a name goodly Orestes made for himself among men, when he killed the man who killed his father? Guileful Aegisthus, who cut down his famous father.
translations.storybook:
Then off you go to look for news of your father. Perhaps somebody will tell you something. Or perhaps you will catch a rumour that comes from Zeus himself, for rumours travel further among people than anything else.

Go to Pylos first, and ask good old Nestor. Then go on to Sparta, to fair-haired Menelaus, who was the very last of the bronze-clad Achaeans to get home.

If you hear that your father is alive and on his way, then be patient just one more year, tired as you are. But if you hear that he is dead and gone, then come home to your own dear country. Build a great mound for him, and hold a proper funeral with all the rites, exactly as it should be done. And then give your mother away to a new husband.

And when all of that is finished, you must think very hard indeed, and work out how you are going to kill the suitors in your own hall. By a clever trick, or right out in the open. It does not matter which. You are far too old now to go on behaving like a little boy.

Have you not heard what a splendid name Orestes won for himself all over the world, when he killed the man who murdered his father? That was sly Aegisthus, who had cut down his famous father.

murray.text: and from thence to Sparta to fair-haired Menelaus; for he was the last to reach home of the brazen-coated Achaeans. If so be thou shalt hear that thy father is alive and coming home, then verily, though thou art sore afflicted, thou couldst endure for yet a year. But if thou shalt hear that he is dead and gone,

then return to thy dear native land and heap up a mound for him, and over it pay funeral rites, full many as is due, and give thy mother to a husband. Then when thou hast done all this and brought it to an end, thereafter take thought in mind and heart

how thou mayest slay the wooers in thy halls whether by guile or openly; for it beseems thee not to practise childish ways, since thou art no longer of such an age. Or hast thou not heard what fame the goodly Orestes won among all mankind when he slew his father's murderer,

the guileful Aegisthus, for that he slew his glorious father? Thou too, my friend, for I see that thou art comely and tall, be thou valiant, that many an one of men yet to be born may praise thee. But now I will go down to my swift ship and my comrades, who, methinks, are chafing much at waiting for me.
